source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
applying for administrative reconsideration and legal remedies exist via
administrative litigation in court.98 Yet, eg, the aspect of ‘naming and
shaming’ can unfold eﬀect beyond the speciﬁc perpetrator making it
diﬃcult to uphold legal principles such as nullum crimen sine lege.
Another hindrance to entities wishing to invoke legal remedies is the lack
of transparency and clarity.99 With regard to blacklists not all entries are
open for reconsideration or restoration. Some entries require a certain pub-
licity period to pass (between several months to ﬁve years) before the
company can apply for credit restoration.100 Hence, it does not seem that
there are eﬀective remedies that match the intrusiveness of the SCS and
that prevent unfair treatment but that there are rather ways to ask for
mercy in order to obtain another chance on the Chinese market.101 Uncer-
tain prospects of eﬀective legal remedies enhance, once more, the incentive
to adopt the values and rationale of the CSCS pre-emptively which fosters the
notion that this is simply common sense: Capitalist common sense incorpor-
ating state-capitalist common sense.
    In consequence, companies might face two options: Attaining high scores
or risking lower scores and losing economic and ﬁnancial access as well as
government support and public services.102 To attain good scores a
company has to fulﬁl the requirements of the CSCS, some of which are trans-
parent and others vague and easy to instrumentalise.103 The same is true for
sanctions and beneﬁts which are sometimes only vaguely described.104 This
creates leeway for discretion and assessment according to political aims and a
corporation might have to incorporate a broader value system in their inter-
and intra-corporate practices.
    Several CSCS features foster the values and interests of the CCP towards
several actors so that present knowledge structures regarding corporate gov-
ernance can be altered and alternative claims to knowledge, right and power
are strengthened in their authority. The private is politicised and used as a
tool to include new aspects in the current common sense of transnational
corporate regulation. Nevertheless, it is not certain that corporations will
act according to this new common sense as they could be faced with conﬂ-
icting obligations or incentives and the remaining old common sense at least
as far as their activities are subject to US and EU inﬂuence.105 In the
